subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question: A fair coin is tossed once. What is the probability of getting a head?
explanation: A fair coin has 2 outcomes: Head or Tail. P(Head) = Favorable outcomes / Total outcomes = 1/2.
formula: P(E) = Number of favorable outcomes / Total number of possible outcomes
concept: Probability measures the likelihood of an event. For a fair coin, each outcome is equally likely.
hint: Count favorable outcomes (1 head) and total outcomes (2: head or tail).
commonMistake: Thinking probability is 1/3 (head, tail, or edge). A coin toss only has 2 meaningful outcomes.
memoryTrick: Coin toss: 2 outcomes, equally likely. P = 1/2. Think: 'One side out of two faces.'

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question: A die is thrown once. What is the probability of getting an even number?
explanation: Even numbers on a die: 2, 4, 6 (3 outcomes). Total outcomes: 6. P(Even) = 3/6 = 1/2.
concept: A standard die has 6 faces (1-6). Even numbers are 2, 4, 6 — exactly half of all outcomes.
hint: Count even numbers on a die (2, 4, 6) and divide by total faces (6).
commonMistake: Counting only 2 and 4 as even (forgetting 6), giving 2/6 = 1/3.
memoryTrick: Die: 3 even (2,4,6), 3 odd (1,3,5). P(even) = 3/6 = 1/2. Think: 'Half the faces are even.'

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question: Two coins are tossed simultaneously. What is the probability of getting exactly one head?
explanation: Sample space for two coins: {HH, HT, TH, TT} = 4 outcomes. Exactly one head: {HT, TH} = 2 outcomes. P(Exactly 1 head) = 2/4 = 1/2.
concept: When tossing two coins, there are 4 equally likely outcomes. 'Exactly one head' means one head and one tail (HT or TH).
hint: List all possible outcomes: HH, HT, TH, TT. Count those with exactly one head.
commonMistake: Thinking outcomes are {0 heads, 1 head, 2 heads} = 3 outcomes, so P = 1/3. These are NOT equally likely!
memoryTrick: Two coins: 4 outcomes (HH, HT, TH, TT). 'Exactly 1 head' = HT, TH = 2/4 = 1/2. Think: 'HT and TH are the mixed pairs.'

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question: A card is drawn from a well-shuffled deck of 52 cards. What is the probability of drawing a face card (Jack, Queen, or King)?
explanation: Face cards: Jack, Queen, King in each of 4 suits = 3 × 4 = 12 cards. P(Face card) = 12/52 = 3/13.
formula: P(Face card) = Number of face cards / Total cards = 12/52 = 3/13
concept: A standard deck has 52 cards with 4 suits. Each suit has 3 face cards (J, Q, K).
hint: Count face cards: 3 per suit × 4 suits = 12. Divide by 52.
commonMistake: Counting Ace as a face card (16/52 = 4/13). Face cards are only J, Q, K — not Ace.
memoryTrick: Face cards = J, Q, K (3 per suit). 3 × 4 = 12. P = 12/52 = 3/13. Think: 'Jack, Queen, King — the royal family, 3 per kingdom.'

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question: Two dice are thrown simultaneously. What is the probability of getting a sum of 7?
explanation: Total outcomes = 6 × 6 = 36. Favorable outcomes for sum 7: (1,6), (2,5), (3,4), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1) = 6 outcomes. P(Sum=7) = 6/36 = 1/6.
formula: P(Sum=7) = 6/36 = 1/6. There are 6 ways to get sum 7 out of 36 total outcomes.
concept: When two dice are thrown, there are 36 equally likely outcomes. Sum of 7 is the most probable sum with 6 combinations.
commonMistake: Thinking there are 7 possible sums (2-12) so P = 1/7. These sums are NOT equally likely!
memoryTrick: Sum of 7: 6 combinations out of 36. P = 6/36 = 1/6. Think: '7 is the luckiest number — most combinations (6 ways) give sum 7.'

subject: Quantitative Aptitude
question: A bag contains 4 red balls, 3 blue balls, and 2 green balls. Two balls are drawn at random without replacement. What is the probability that both balls are of the same color?
explanation: Total balls = 9. Total ways to draw 2 = C(9,2) = 36. Same color: Both red = C(4,2) = 6. Both blue = C(3,2) = 3. Both green = C(2,2) = 1. Total favorable = 6 + 3 + 1 = 10. P = 10/36 = 5/18.
concept: When drawing without replacement, use combinations to count favorable and total outcomes. Sum probabilities for each color.
hint: Calculate combinations for each color pair: C(4,2) for red, C(3,2) for blue, C(2,2) for green. Sum and divide by C(9,2).
commonMistake: Adding individual probabilities: 4/9 × 3/9 + 3/9 × 2/9 + 2/9 × 1/9. This uses WITH replacement formula instead of without replacement.
memoryTrick: Without replacement: Use combinations C(n,2). Same color = sum of same-color pairs / total pairs. Think: 'Pick 2 from same group, add all groups, divide by all possible pairs.'
